What’s in a Name: Home Again At the more anthropological level of dwelling practices, one can observe another phenomenon which surely relates to the increasing speed with which 3 Evidently enough, the instigation of desire for a better and bigger and cooler house equally works through the internet, where we can click around and have a look at houses for sale. All larger newspapers now have housing market search engines on their websites. Liminality and the Modern 224 we change dwellings. Up until quite recently, houses in many parts of Europe were given a name, a fact well-documented by folklorists. As Simmel noted, ‘The house that is called by its own name [gives] its inhabitants a feeling of spatial individuality, of belonging to a qualitatively fixed point in space. Through the name associated with it, the house forms a much more autonomous, individually nuanced existence’ (Simmel quoted in Keohane 2013: 65). One is reminded of one of Bourdieu’s best writings, namely his structural analysis of the Kabyle house where he shows how the house serves as a spatial-semantic ordering of a moral universe (Bourdieu 1970). This is not particular to the Kabyle: in most cultural settings we know off, the house is imbued with meaningfulness in each of its material manifestations. In Denmark, where he who writes grew up, one can still see these names on houses, especially in towns and villages. Trivial as such names are, they testify to an understanding of a house as having a soul and identity, and often also a history connected to that of a family. The specific names used for houses often also refer to a value. In Denmark, most of the names given to houses during the nineteenth century belonged to one of the following six categories (Porsmose 2008: 152): 1. Names indicating hope connected to fertility (Heldbo, house of luck). 2. Names indicating a particular civic virtue (Nøjsomhed, humbleness). 3. Names signalling a reward or a wish for a good, quiet life (Fredenshjem, home of peace). 4. Names indicating satisfaction (Lykkebo, life in happiness). 5. Patriotic names (Danebo, house of Danes). 6. Names indicating a beautiful location (Skøndal, beautiful valley or Stella Maris, star of the seas). In most European locations, house-naming anticipated by centuries the naming and numbering of streets, indicating the centrality of houses in spatial and semantic worldmaking. It is no coincidence that royal families in Europe recognized and bespoke themselves as ‘houses’: the house was the primary spatial substance from where the family genealogy could unfold in history. The ‘dynasty’ referred to the power of the ruler (from the Greek, dynasthai, to ‘have power’), but the self- identification of the royal family remained tied to the notion of a house. Such house-naming practices have a deep and important history. In many parts of the world, newly built houses are baptized just like persons in a ritual celebration. In Rites of Passage Arnold van Gennep starts his empirical discussion of liminality and ritual passages with a careful consideration of such practices, as ‘the sacrifices associated with laying the foundation for a house and constructing a house fall into the category of rites of passage’ (1960: 23). Ceremonies connected to the first dwelling of a house include rites of separation, the ritual lifting of a taboo, and rites of incorporation: a ceremonial visiting, a consecration of the various parts of the building (in many Catholic countries this is still today done by the local priest who sprinkles the walls with holy water while reciting from By Way of Conclusion: Out of Liminality 225 the Bible), and often the sharing of salt and bread, or any other symbolic meal. These ceremonies, notes van Gennep, ‘are essentially rites identifying the future inhabitants with their new residence’ (ibid.: 24). In many cases, this ritual is seen as the last ritual of reintegration closing the ritual sequence of the marriage ceremony; the widespread European tradition that the groom has to carry the bride over the threshold of the front door resumes its full significance within this ceremonial whole. We cannot possibly underestimate the significance of all this; in fact, as van Gennep opens his next chapter, Individuals and Groups, ‘A society is similar to a house divided into rooms and corridors’ (van Gennep 1960: 26). Possibly going beyond what van Gennep meant to imply here, this is because any meaningful formation of public life must flow from the oikos, from how we dwell. This recognition implies something quite different from the ‘protection’ of our private and public spheres of life from market mechanisms and state power, as Habermas wants it; it is rather the recognition, formulated with such great clarity by Aristotle, and Plato before him, that the order of society must rest upon the order of the soul. In Ancient Greece, as Simmel reminds us, to sell a family home or a landed property was ‘an offence not only against the children, but also against the ancestors, because it disrupted the continuity of the family’ (Simmel 2011: 259; see again Keohane 2013).The house and its fence protected man against enemies and other dangers. Its divine protector was Zeus. The Greek word for fence was herkos, and herkeios is an epithet of Zeus. According to Homer, the altar of Zeus Herkeios generally stood in the courtyard before the house, where sacrifices and libations were offered to him. Even more important and interesting is another form of Zeus in which he appears as a ‘house god’, Zeus Ktesios. This is not much discussed by ancient historians, maybe because the simple house cult belonged to those daily routines that quite often do not find their way into our written records. The hearth was the centre of the house cult and of the piety of daily life (see Nilsson 1940). The Greek word denoting the larger household was of course oikos. 4 As van Gennep was well-aware, such rites connected to dwelling cannot be catalogued as superstition: they are distillations of centuries,
